other_observers: Nick Komar, Charles Thornton-Kolbe, and about 10 others birders.; Nick Komar is the original finder and identifier of the bird.
specific_location: Gregory Canyon, 125 yards up the Saddle Rock Trail at 39.997436N, 105.294635W
description: A small Wren with a small dark bill and a very short, persistently upwardly cocked tail. The entire bird had a rich rufous coloring. The supercilium was buff colored and prominent, yet low contrast with the rest of the head. The chest was also buff colored and nearly the same tone as the remainder of the bird.Behaviors: The bird was scurrying on the ground in dense thicket, rarely flying up more than 6 inches from the ground cover. The bird responded to pishing by hopping up higher on small twigs where it remained briefly (< 10 seconds) before continuing to forage along the ground. The bird was located by following its calls, but the call frequency (calls per minute) was low, although not measured. At one point, the bird approached a group of nearly 10 bird watchers to a distance of nearly 2 feet and was close enough to me that I was unable to focus my binoculars.Call: The bird was initially located by Nick Komar by its Warbler like call. We followed the calling bird until Nick was able to visually identify it. We quickly realized that the bird was not a Warbler but a Winter Wren. This Warbler-like call most resembled a chip note from an Oporornis or Wilsonia Warbler and was unlike the chip note from Song Sparrow.Plumage: adult
